title: Demo Day & Graduation
subtitle: Two weeks. Ten days. One showcase. Your AI future begins now.
overview: This is the day everything comes together. You will present your project to a real audience — parents, teachers, or guests — and demonstrate not just what you built, but how you think.

This session is also a celebration of how far you have come in two weeks. By the end of today you will have presented with confidence, demonstrated your project live, written a letter to your future self, received your certificate, and left with a clear next step for continuing your AI learning.
objectives: Deliver your 3-minute project presentation with confidence
Demonstrate your project to the audience live
Complete your "Letter to Future Self" — a personal commitment
Receive your AI Futures Program certificate
Leave with a clear next step for continuing your AI learning

concepts.heading: The Demo Day Mindset
body: A great demo is more about how you tell the story than how perfect the project is. Judges and guests remember presenters who make them care about the problem and who speak honestly about what they learned.
bullets: Start with the problem, not the technology. The audience needs to care before they can be impressed.
Show the tool working live — even if it's imperfect. A live demo with one bug is more compelling than a flawless recording.
Speak to what you learned, not just what you built. Judges value insight over polish.
If something goes wrong during the demo, say so calmly and explain what it should do. Handling failure gracefully is itself impressive.
Thank everyone who helped you and gave you feedback while you built and tested it.

text: A live demo with one bug beats a flawless recording — it shows the real thing works.
If something breaks, stay calm and explain what it should do. Handling failure well is impressive on its own.

concepts.heading: What Comes Next — Your AI Learning Path
body: Your AI journey doesn't end today. Here are free, trustworthy resources to keep learning, building, and thinking about AI responsibly.
bullets: Coding with AI — CS50's Introduction to AI (Harvard, free): cs50.harvard.edu/ai
Coding with AI — Elements of AI (University of Helsinki, free): elementsofai.com
Coding with AI — Scratch + ML for Kids: machinelearningforkids.co.uk
Building with AI — Google ML Crash Course (free): developers.google.com/machine-learning/crash-course
Building with AI — Fast.ai Practical Deep Learning (free): fast.ai
Building with AI — Anthropic Claude API (free tier): anthropic.com/api
Understanding AI Ethics — AI4K12 Initiative: ai4k12.org
Understanding AI Ethics — MIT Media Lab Ethics of AI: media.mit.edu
Understanding AI Ethics — Your Digital Future (Australian eSafety Commissioner): esafety.gov.au
Competitions and Communities — AI for Good (ITU): aiforgood.itu.int/youth
Competitions and Communities — Google Science Fair: sciencefair.withgoogle.com
Competitions and Communities — iD Tech AI programs (holidays): idtech.com

activities.name: Project Showcase
intro: Each team presents their 3-minute project to the audience, followed by 2 minutes of questions. This is your moment — tell your story and show your project working live.
steps: Set up your project so it's ready to show.
Give your 3-minute demo: the problem, your solution, how you used AI, and what you learned.
Try the audience questions on your own first — could you answer them?
Watch other projects and leave one kind, specific piece of feedback.
Thank everyone who helped you and tried out your project.
facilitator: Keep a strict timer: 3 minutes to present, 2 minutes for Q&A per team.
Confirm every team's tech is working during final setup, before any audience arrives.

activities.name: Letter to My Future Self
intro: Take 15 quiet minutes to write a letter to your future self. Seal it and set a reminder to open it in about 6 months — right in the middle of a new school year, a message from the you who just finished this adventure.
steps: Right now, here is what I understand about AI that I didn't two weeks ago:
The project I built was called [name]. Here is what I'm most proud of about it:
The moment that surprised me most during this program was:
The ethical question about AI that I still think about is:
By the time you read this letter, I want to have done these things with AI:
The one thing I want you to remember about AI is:
facilitator: Provide each student with a piece of quality paper and an envelope.
Allow 15 minutes of quiet, reflective writing. Play soft instrumental music.
Students seal and address the envelope to themselves; facilitators mail them in 6 months.
Tell students: "This letter will arrive when you are in the middle of a new school year. It is a message from the version of you who just finished this program."

reflection: Right now, what do you understand about AI that you didn't two weeks ago?
What are you most proud of about the project you built?
What moment surprised you most during this program?
What ethical question about AI do you still think about?
What is the one thing you want to remember about AI — and what's your next step to keep learning?

vocab.definition: Showing your project actually working in front of an audience, in real time.
Question-and-answer time where the audience asks about your project.
A short, persuasive presentation of your project and why it matters.

quiz.q: According to the Demo Day mindset, how should you open your presentation?
options: With the problem, so the audience cares before they can be impressed
With a long technical description of how the AI works
With a list of every tool you used
With an apology for anything that might break
quiz.explain: Start with the problem, not the technology. The audience needs to care about the problem before your solution can impress them.

quiz.q: A live demo with one small bug is more compelling than a perfect pre-recorded video.
quiz.explain: True. Showing the real tool working live — even imperfectly — is more convincing and impressive than a flawless recording.

quiz.q: If something goes wrong during your live demo, what's the best thing to do?
options: Stay calm, say what happened, and explain what it should do
Stop the presentation and sit down
Pretend nothing happened and rush past it
Blame the computer and give up
quiz.explain: Handling failure gracefully is itself impressive. Calmly explain what went wrong and what the tool should do — judges respect that.

quiz.q: Judges value polish more than the insights you share about what you learned.
quiz.explain: False. Speak to what you learned, not just what you built — judges value insight over polish.

facilitator.heading: Audience Questions — Facilitator Guide
body: Suggested questions to offer audience members and guests during each team's Q&A:
What made you choose this particular problem to solve?
How did you decide which parts of the solution to give to AI and which to keep for humans?
What would happen if someone tried to misuse your tool?
If you had 3 more weeks, what would you build next?
What did AI do that surprised you during this project?
What did you discover that you wish you'd known before you started building?

facilitator.heading: Award Categories
body: Audience Choice Awards — all teams are eligible for all categories. Guests vote on each.

table.caption: Audience Choice Awards
rows: Tackled a problem in an unexpected way
Easiest and most enjoyable to use
Could help the most people if fully built
Most thoughtful and effective use of AI capabilities
Best handling of fairness, privacy, and safety concerns
Clearest storytelling and most engaging delivery
Final setup — test all projects, arrange exhibition space
Opening ceremony — facilitator reflects on the two-week journey
Project Showcase — each team presents 3 min + 2 min Q&A
Audience choice awards — guests vote on categories
Letter to Future Self — quiet writing time
Closing words from facilitator: what comes next?
